Memorandum

To      :    File                                          Date    :    December 17, 1985
                                                                        No. M-85-215


From  :  FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
          Diane Maura Fishburn

Subject :  Disposition


          I had several phone conversations with Mr. Curry regarding
his request for advice. I gave him informal assistance and
information but declined to provide advice since he did not
represent the affected group. We agreed that I would not send
him a written response.

OFFICE OF THE

CITY ATTORNEY

LIVERMORE, CALIFORNIA

THOMAS R. CURRY
City Attorney

1052 S. Livermore Ave.
Livermore, California 94550
(415) 449-4018

October 7, 1985


Legal Division of Fair Political
Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: Request for Advice on Definition of Committee

Dear Sir or Madam:

I am writing to request your advice on the question of
whether a particular local organization is a committee within the
definition of the Political Reform Act and thus subject to the
various regulations concerning committees which are contained in
that Act.

Approximately two years ago an organization was formed
in Livermore entitled Citizens Advocating Planned Progress (CAPP).
CAPP, among other activities, publishes and distributes the
"Livermore Newsletter". For your convenience I have attached
copies of various representative issues of the Livermore Newsletter.
As you can see, the newsletter has taken positions urging certain
action in connection with various local elections (e.g. support of
Measure K and support for various candidates for the Livermore City
Council).

The City of Livermore has enacted a local ordinance
regulating election campaign contributions. Our local ordinance
adopts for the most part the definitions contained in the Political
Reform Act. With particular regard to the definition of committee,
our local ordinance adds to the definition of committee found in
the Political Reform Act the following:

"...(1) A Committee, person or group of persons organized
for the purpose or charged with the duty of conducting or aiding the
election campaign, including fund raising events, of any candidate
for municipal office of the City or for the support or defeat of a
measure under consideration in the City;

(2) Any committee, person or group of persons aiding,
directly or indirectly, any candidate, measure or committee, as
defined in Subsection B(1) above, whether or not originally organized
for election purposes."

Legal Division of Fair Political
Practices Commission
Re: CAPP
October 7, 1985
Page 2


Your advice in this matter is very much appreciated.

Sincerely yours,

[signature]
THOMAS R. CURRY
City Attorney

Pro-growth group
seeks to get word
out on Livermore

By Katherine Conrad
Times staff writer

LIVERMORE — A group that bills itself as representing the silent majority has decided to exercise its right to free speech by publishing a pro-growth monthly newsletter aimed at giving residents the "whole truth" before November's City Council election.

"The biggest reason we're putting out the newsletter is we would like for people to hear the whole truth and both sides of the story," said Janice Cargo, board member and past president of Citizens Advocating Planning Progress, which was organized a year ago to support the sewage expansion proposal, Measure K, which was approved by voters.

In the past, the group has taken stands against City Council members Cathie Brown, Marshall Kamena and John Stein and in favor of Mayor Dale Turner and Councilman Ayn Wieskamp. The group supports reviewing the general plan, easing controls on business and backing Livermore's participation in the long-term sewage project initiated by Pleasanton and the Dublin San Ramon Services District.

Cargo complained that CAPP members have little access to the press and are rarely quoted while, she said, comments from activists such as Don Miller, a former mayor who has sued the city over development approvals, do appear in print.

"We've had victories in Measure K and we succeeded in retaining the mayor's powers," she said. "We are the silent majority and we want to be heard. We want people to know there is another voice."

The lead article for the publication dated July 27 bears the headline, "Will the last business leaving Livermore please turn off the lights?" and comments that 572 businesses either closed or moved elsewhere in the last year. At the end of the article, it says that more than 600 new businesses have opened their doors during the same period.

City government is held responsible and Kamena, Stein and Brown are blamed for "continuing the anti-business, anti-growth policies of the Don Miller clique," the article states.

Brown responded that the City Council has worked hard to adopt redevelopment standards that will improve the downtown. She also noted that several major companies have moved to Livermore in the past year, including K Mart, Miller's Outpost and Triad Systems Inc., which plans to build its headquarters and computer training complex on 400 acres near the airport.

Kamena said the article distorted the economic picture of Livermore, which just passed a $24 million municipal budget, the biggest in the city's history.

"If one looks at the newsletter and has no acquaintance with the city, one might think it's a bleak place to life," he said. "But if one cares to come here and look at the activity and the small-town atmosphere, which should be preserved, one would conclude it's a dynamic, attractive place."

The publication is mailed to 10,000 Livermore homes. The first issue appeared a year ago, said Denise Howe, another board member.

Pro-development newsletter
'We've been ignored'

By Allan Hirsch
Staff writer

LIVERMORE — A group desiring stronger pro-development policies in this city has decided to speak directly to the people by mailing a newsletter to about 7,500 registered voters.

The premiere edition of the "Livermore Newsletter," published by the 250-member Citizens Advocating Planned Progress, reports that "the ability to attract and keep businesses in Livermore city government" was a factor cited by a number of business owners who have left Livermore in the past year.

Members of the 2½-year-old group said they decided to publish the monthly newsletter because their views have not been reported in local newspapers.

"We've been ignored for 2½ years," said Livermore interior designer Janice Cargo, a director and former president of the group.

Larry Chesney, the group's current president, said, "We want to present some facts, not brought out by the local papers, that are pertinent to the voters of Livermore."

According to the newsletter, when a former (and former mayor) Don Miller gets up at a City Council meeting, he gets 2½ columns of press. But when five or six people stand up supporting a project, they may get a mention," Chesney said.

The newsletter, mailed earlier this week, will be published monthly until the City Council elections in November, according to Cargo. The group wants to continue publishing the newsletter after the elections if the council approves permits.

The group's directors, however, hope their newsletter and candidates will win council seats in the coming election, Chesney acknowledged.

Citizens Advocating Planned Progress was originally founded, according to the newsletter, because members were not reviewing the 2 percent annual growth limit that has existed in Livermore since the 1970s. Members of the group are a mix of Livermore residents and business people.

The group favors a review of the city's growth policies, more affordable housing and a stronger pro-business policy by the city.

Last year the group supported Measure R, a ballot measure that approved a residential development system. The group also helped in a successful referendum campaign that preserved the mayor's appointment powers, and has supported the construction of shopping facilities in Springtown, according to Chesney.

[IMAGE: Black and white photograph of Larry Chesney wearing glasses]

The lead story in this month's newsletter quotes several former Livermore business owners who have moved to other cities. The owners are quoted as saying the city government does not support business. Mayor Dale Turner, considered one of the stronger supporters of growth on the council, called the story "very misleading" and said it "borders on irresponsibility."

Turner argued that many business people feel Dublin and north Pleasanton are more centrally located than Livermore. "The city is concerned about any businesses that leave. These things aren't taken lightly. We want good economy in the valley," he said.

Another story in the newsletter deals with City Council candidate Tom Vargas' support of Livermore's new anti-cruising ordinance. Cargo said she is Vargas' campaign manager.

Resident uses new technology to fly at night

By Staff Sergeant Joe Edwards

Robert Campos of Livermore learned to fly in the dark last month at the Army's Aviation Center in the United States. Army Reserve Commander the Aviation Section of the 9ist Division during recent helicopter night flying exercises.

"Night flying is different from day flying," said the pilot in addition to commanding the section 14 soldiers including seven pilots, pronounced the two weeks of night flying super training a success.

"We reached our goal the first week to reach depths required to have proficiency," Campos said. "We flew with goggles in addition to classroom instruction and testing. The second week we flew with goggles below ridges, as low as possible at about 50 miles an hour, which will be interesting," Campos said.

The goggles make flying in the dark as easy as flying in the day, the older method of winging it in tuck and a prayer. They amplify available light so a pilot can see well enough to pilot the UH1H helicopter (Hueys, as they are commonly called) down safely even on the darkest nights. Still, the task can be unforgiving. "On one flight I went down in the darkness at 200 feet above the earth! As a passenger on the flight without the goggles, I thought I was setting down in a bottle of black ink," and down it went since they have only a limited 40-degree field of vision to the front of the aircraft. The limitation in the angle of the goggles to the front of the pilot's head also tends to cause headaches.

Camps said, "The U.S. Army has made night goggles a requirement in the last year, and everyone has to qualify.... Safety is always on the list though. It always comes first. We have regulations and we go by them. By the end of annual training, we'll be there that second aircraft is qualified."

The Sixth Army contains reserve units in 12 Western States. "Camps is a combat veteran who served on active duty 10 hours of combat flying time with the first battel Air cavalry in Thua Valley during the Vietnam era. He's also a instructor pilot of the Contra Costa Fire Department, where he has been working for more than 13 years.

"His aviation section's mission is to provide support for the 91st Division, a training division with reserve units at Camp Parks in Dublin as well as other locations from Redding to Fresno and from Ukiah to the Nevada border. They train for two days a month and two weeks of annual training, usually during the summer.

Vargas, Bartoli challenge incumbents

An unusually large pack of candidates for the Livermore City Council has divided the city election into two distinct races.

The early campaign was frenetic, even bitter between the two camps battling to emerge as the front-runner among eight challengers for the seats of the retiring Councilmen. In Tuesday's election, John Stein and Marshall Kamena, the second campaign, benefited from the clearly ideological battle between front-runners progressive and slow-growth incumbents.

Tom Vargas, a Livermore business man and an early entry of the early campaign, with office manager Jack Bartoli following close by biding, it was a come-from-behind victory for both, for each began by building a strong base of support from as former City Councilmember Marlin Ebert, former school board member Jan Brice, and Livermore Area Regional Park District Board member Tom Hendricks.

ANALYSIS

The Livermore Poll

"Do You Approve of the City's Smoking Ban?"

The Livermore Poll begins today as a regular feature examining points of interest to the news in Livermore. Callers are invited to call and respond to the poll question listed below. The results will be printed in next month's edition of The Livermore Newsletter, along with brief explanations. The Livermore Poll is a non-scientific compilation of readers' opinions.

Today's question: "Do you approve of the City's Smoking Ban?"
refers to Livermore City Council ordinance regulating office smoking that goes into effect August 9. The ordinance requires employers to designate "non-smoking areas" in their offices for non-smoking workers, and develops smoking rules. Each employee has the right to work in a non-smoking area if one is available. Smoking is also banned in theaters, banks, retail stores, public lines and waiting rooms within the City limits. Restaurants must designate at least half of their tables as non-smoking areas. Fines of up to $500 can be imposed on violators for repeat offenses.

Supporters of the smoking ban, which was unanimously approved by the City Council in June, say that smoke is harmful to people with health problems and offensive to most other non-smokers. Some say the ban will reduce fire hazards.

Opponents among the Livermore business community, however, recommended a six-month voluntary no-smoking ban. The voluntary guidelines would recommend restricting smoking to certain areas, and results would have been monitored by a city task force.

Opponents say the ban will be difficult to enforce by employers and may strain the already overworked police force. Supporters say the ordinance will be largely self-enforcing, since police will only respond to citizen complaints rather than searching for violations in the workplace. Police maintain that a voluntary ordinance would be ignored by smokers and employers.

We'd like to hear your opinions by calling our office hours by August 1. Responses will be kept confidential unless you specifically permit us to quote you by name.

Tips to Protect your Home

PLAN & PROTECT

Experience indicates that most break-ins are committed by neighborhood teenagers who sneak in through an unlocked door or window. Making it just a little tougher to get in might prevent the casual burglar from entering your home.

Upgrading the locks on your home with keys—and it costs relatively little—can make a big difference and is otherwise easy. Any reputable locksmith can advise you on the best locks.

Alarm systems are becoming increasingly popular. It's estimated that an alarm system runs in the neighborhood of $2,000.

If you can't afford an alarm system, there are other, less expensive approaches to home security.

Outdoor Lighting. Lights near doorways are a good idea, as the removal of dark shadows makes it easier for a burglar to hide, and might provide a dark hiding place.

Indoor Lighting. One or two lights burning inside your home when someone is home. A timing device which turns on lights when dark is especially effective.

Leave the radio on when you are gone. If someone comes to your door and hears the sound of a radio, he can't be sure if you're home.

A barking dog's refusal to prevent intruders bad news. Since every time thieves will be using the dog to determine if the house is empty and to see if someone is home, the sound of a dog barking could scare them off when one comes to the door.

Not knowing if the dog is vicious or friendly, the burglar might try another house. In addition to that, he will not want to give attention to himself with the noise.

The Livermore Poll begins today as a regular feature examining 
topics of interest in the news in livermore. Callers are invited to 
register their opinions on the seleded topic by calling the numbers 
listed below. The results will be printed in next month's edition of The 
Livermore Newsletter, along with a few representative comments. The 
Livermore Poll is a non-scientific compilation of readers' opinions. 
Today's question, "Do you approve of the City's Smoking Ban?" 
refers to a livermore City Council ordinance regulating office smoking 
that goes into effed August 9. The ordinance requires employers to 
designate "non-smoking areas" in their offices for non-smoking 
workers and develop smoking rules. Each employee has the right to 
ban smoking in his or her individual work area. 
Smoking is also banned in theaters, banks, retail stores, public Jines 
and waiting rooms within the city limits. Restaurants must designate at 
least half of their tables as non-smoking areas. fines of up to $500 can 
be imposed on violators for repeat offenses.

Supporters of the smoking ban, which was unanimously approved 
by the City Council in June, say that smoke is harmful to people with 
health problems and offensive to most other non-smokers. Some 
claim that "second-hand" tobacco smoke can even kill. 
Opponents among the livermore business community, however, 
recommended a six-month, voluntary no-smoking plan. The voluntary 
guidelines would recommend restriding smoking to certain areas, and 
results would have been monitored by a city task force.

ordinance will be largely self-enforcing, since police will only respond 
to citizen complaints rather than searching for violation, in the work-
place. They maintain that a voluntary ordinance would be ignored by 
smokers and employers.

sections.question_synthetic: The City Attorney of Livermore seeks FPPC advice on whether Citizens Advocating Planned Progress (CAPP), a local organization that publishes a newsletter taking positions on local elections and candidates, meets the definition of a political committee under the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: This file contains the initial incoming request from the City Attorney (October 7, 1985), a follow-up letter (October 25, 1985), newspaper articles about CAPP and the Livermore Newsletter, and an internal FPPC memorandum (December 17, 1985) indicating informal advice was given but no written response was sent. No formal FPPC advice letter or conclusion is present in this document set.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether a particular local organization (Citizens Advocating Planned Progress - CAPP) is a committee within the definition of the Political Reform Act and thus subject to the various regulations concerning committees which are contained in that Act.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Memorandum To : File Date : December 17, 1985 No. M-85-215 From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Diane Maura Fishburn Subject : Disposition I had several phone conversations with Mr. Curry regarding his request for advice. I gave him informal assistance and information but declined to provide advice since he did not represent the affected group. We agreed that I would not send him a written response. OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY LIVERMORE, CALIFORNIA THOMAS R. CURRY City Attorney 1052 S. Livermore Ave. Livermore, California 94550 (415) 449-4018 October 7, 1985 Legal Division of Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. Box 807 Sacramento, California 95804 Re: Request for Advice on Definition of Committee Dear Sir or Madam: I am writing to request your advice on the question of whether a particular local organization is a committee within the definition of the Political Reform Act and thus subject to the various regulations concerning committees which are contained in that Act. Approximately two years ago an organization was formed in Livermore entitled Citizens Advocating Planned Progress (CAPP). CAPP, among other activities, publishes and distributes the "Livermore Newsletter". For your convenience I have attached copies of various representative issues of the Livermore Newsletter. As you can see, the newsletter has taken positions urging certain action in connection with various local elections (e.g. support of Measure K and support for various candidates for the Livermore City Council). The City of Livermore has enacted a local ordinance regulating election campaign contributions. Our local ordinance adopts for the most part the definitions contained in the Political Reform Act. With particular regard to the definition of committee, our local ordinance adds to the definition of committee found in the Political Reform Act the following: "...(1) A Committee, person or group of persons organized for the purpose or charged with the duty of conducting or aiding the election campaign, including fund raising events, of any candidate for municipal office of the City or for the support or defeat of a measure under consideration in the City; (2) Any committee, person or group of persons aiding, directly or indirectly, any candidate, measure or committee, as defined in Subsection B(1) above, whether or not originally organized for election purposes." Legal Division of Fair Political Practices Commission Re: CAPP October 7, 1985 Page 2 Your advice in this matter is very much appreciated. Sincerely yours, [signature] THOMAS R. embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from the City Attorney of Livermore (Thomas R. Curry) seeking FPPC advice on whether CAPP, a pro-growth local organization that publishes the Livermore Newsletter advocating positions on candidates and measures, qualifies as a political committee under the Political Reform Act. The accompanying memorandum indicates the FPPC provided only informal assistance by phone and declined to issue a formal advice letter because the requester did not represent the affected group.
